map.subtitle: Big Idea 3: Algorithms and Programming   •   1 day   •   LO AAP-4.B
map.intro: Everything for this topic lives in this folder. Topic 3.18 is the CAPSTONE of Big Idea 3 — a conceptual, 'explain'-only topic with no coding practice — so its practice is two paper exercises (classify and explain-why) rather than a coding page. Here is how the surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Day 1 (decidable problems; undecidable problems; the exclusion; solvable instances; the two limits of computing). Speaker notes on every slide.
Teacher Guide (one-day pacing with the QOTD warm-up, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Day 1 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Decidable or Not? and Exercise 2: Explain Why (student + answer KEY) — both PAPER classify/explain tasks, no coding
Discussion — Can Every Question Be Answered by an Algorithm? (3-part: Opening, Mid-lesson, Closing, all within Day 1)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 8 questions on LO AAP-4.B, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the instruction you project and teach from (decidable vs undecidable; the exclusion; some instances solvable but no universal algorithm). This is an 'explain' topic, so all examples — the isEven algorithm, the Halting Problem — are shown to build reasoning, not to be coded.

This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site. Because there is no coding practice for this conceptual capstone, there is no coding-reference key either.
Website = student practice that reinforces the reasoning: the two exercises and the quiz grade instantly with per-answer feedback. Student versions live online; the answer keys stay with you.

Scheduling: Topic 3.18 is a 1-day topic and the final topic of Big Idea 3. The quiz runs as the opener of the NEXT class (spaced retrieval and a Big Idea 3 wrap-up), so the topic never silently consumes a second day. Big Idea 4 (Computer Systems and Networks) begins after the quiz.

exercise1.title: Decidable or Not?
exercise1.intro: You are a 'Yes/No Machine Inspector.' Your job is to look at each decision problem and judge whether ONE algorithm could answer it correctly for every possible input (decidable) or whether it has been established that no such algorithm can exist (undecidable). This is the core skill for LO AAP-4.B: reason precisely from the definitions. Two rules of the job — you apply the definitions to well-established cases, and you are NEVER asked to prove undecidability of a new problem yourself (that is outside the AP scope).
exercise1.scenarioTitle: The Inspector's Log: Five Decision Problems
exercise1.narrative: Read the log top to bottom. For each problem, decide whether a single algorithm could be correct for ALL inputs. Three of these are classic decidable problems you can settle by naming the algorithm. One is the famous problem computer scientists have PROVEN is undecidable — the Halting Problem — which is given to you as an established fact, not something you must prove. The last row looks at a single instance of that undecidable problem, to show the difference between a whole problem and one instance.

columns: One algorithm correct for ALL inputs?
rows: Is a whole number even?
Yes — test whether the number MOD 2 = 0
Decidable
One fixed rule is correct for every integer input
Is a target value in a given list?
Yes — linear search checks each element in order
Decidable
The same search works for any list and any target
Is a given list already in sorted order?
Yes — compare each element to the next one
Decidable
A single pass returns the correct yes/no for any list
For ANY program and input, will it eventually halt (stop)?
No — it is proven that none can exist
Undecidable (the Halting Problem)
No algorithm is correct for all programs; given as an established result
Does the specific program 'DISPLAY(1)' halt?
Yes — read it: it runs one step and stops
A solvable INSTANCE of an undecidable problem
Some instances are decidable even though the whole problem is not

exercise1.scenarioNote: Problems drawn from the Day 1 decidable table and the CED's 'Is the number even?' example and Halting Problem discussion; pseudocode notation follows the AP Computer Science Principles Exam Reference Sheet (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, predict in one sentence: which single row proves that 'undecidable' does NOT mean 'a computer can never answer any version of the question'?

partA.q: Rows 1-3 are all decidable. In general terms, state what ALL decidable problems have in common, and explain why naming an algorithm (like the remainder test in row 1) is what proves a problem decidable.
partA.answer: Every decidable problem is a decision problem (yes/no answer) for which a single algorithm can be written that produces the CORRECT output for all inputs (EK AAP-4.B.1). Naming the algorithm is what proves decidability because it demonstrates that one fixed procedure covers every possible input, not just the cases you happened to test: the remainder test in row 1 correctly reports even/odd for 10, for 7, for a billion, for any integer. Rows 2 and 3 work the same way — linear search and the neighbor-comparison pass each give the right yes/no for any input. Full credit requires the 'for all inputs' idea plus the point that exhibiting one always-correct algorithm is the proof.

partA.q: Row 4 (the Halting Problem) is undecidable. Explain what 'undecidable' means here, and explain why buying a computer a thousand times faster would NOT make this problem decidable.
partA.answer: Undecidable means that no algorithm can be constructed that is always capable of giving a correct yes-or-no answer for every program and input (EK AAP-4.B.2). It is a statement about impossibility, not difficulty: it is not that the algorithm is slow or undiscovered — a correct-for-all-inputs algorithm provably cannot exist. A far faster computer does not help, because the barrier is the non-existence of the algorithm, not the speed of the machine; a faster computer would simply run the same impossible task faster. This is different from a problem that merely takes an unreasonable amount of time (Topic 3.17), where a correct algorithm DOES exist and is only impractically slow.

partA.q: Rows 4 and 5 both ask about halting, yet row 4 is undecidable and row 5 is easily solved. Explain how both statements can be true at the same time.
partA.answer: They operate at different levels — the whole problem versus a single instance. Row 4 is the whole problem: 'for ANY program and input, will it halt?' No single algorithm is correct for all of those inputs, so the problem is undecidable (EK AAP-4.B.2). Row 5 is one particular instance: the specific program DISPLAY(1) obviously runs one step and stops, so this instance has an algorithmic solution. EK AAP-4.B.3 states exactly this: an undecidable problem may have some instances that have an algorithmic solution, but there is no algorithmic solution that could solve ALL instances. So 'undecidable' describes the whole problem; it does not mean every individual case is unsolvable.

partA.q: Suppose an inspector is handed a brand-new, unfamiliar decision problem. According to the AP Computer Science Principles boundaries, what IS the inspector expected to be able to do, and what is explicitly NOT their job?
partA.answer: The inspector is expected to explain what decidable and undecidable mean and to explain that undecidable problems exist in computer science (LO AAP-4.B). What is explicitly NOT their job is determining, on their own, whether that given problem is undecidable — the exclusion on EK AAP-4.B.2 states that determining whether a given problem is undecidable is outside the scope of the course and the AP Exam. In other words, they reason from the definitions and known/established examples, but they never have to derive or prove the undecidability of an unfamiliar problem.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: Write your OWN example of a decidable decision problem that is not already in the log, and name the single algorithm that answers it correctly for every input.
partB.answer: Example: 'Is a given whole number positive?' — one algorithm answers it for all inputs: return whether the number is greater than 0. Any equivalent yes/no problem with a stated always-correct algorithm earns full credit — for instance 'Is a given word a palindrome?' (compare the first and last characters moving inward until they meet or a mismatch is found), or 'Does a given list contain the value 5?' (linear search). Full credit requires (1) a genuine decision problem with a yes/no answer and (2) one algorithm described clearly enough that it is correct for every input (EK AAP-4.B.1), not just for a few examples.

partB.q: Explain the difference between a problem that is UNDECIDABLE and a problem that simply takes an UNREASONABLE amount of time. Give one example of each, and say which one still has a correct algorithm.
partB.answer: An undecidable problem has NO algorithm that is always correct for all inputs (EK AAP-4.B.2) — for example, the Halting Problem. A problem that takes an unreasonable amount of time DOES have a correct algorithm; it is just impractically slow — for example, checking every possible route to find the shortest by brute force grows factorially. The one that still has a correct algorithm is the unreasonable-time problem: it is decidable but intractable. The undecidable problem has no correct-for-all-inputs algorithm at all, so there is nothing to speed up. The key contrast is impossibility (undecidable) versus mere slowness (unreasonable time).

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: A classmate says, 'Since the Halting Problem is undecidable, it is pointless — a computer can't tell us anything about whether programs halt.' Using the idea of solvable instances, explain what is right and what is wrong about this claim.
partB.answer: What is right: there is no single algorithm that correctly decides halting for EVERY program and input, so we cannot have a universal 'will it halt?' tool (EK AAP-4.B.2). What is wrong: 'pointless' and 'can't tell us anything' overstate it. Many individual instances are easy to decide — DISPLAY(1) clearly halts, REPEAT UNTIL(false) clearly loops forever — and in practice tools reason successfully about huge numbers of specific programs. EK AAP-4.B.3 captures this: an undecidable problem may have some instances with an algorithmic solution; only the goal of one algorithm that solves ALL instances is impossible.

partB.q: Next step: you classified and explained these by hand. Now head to the Topic 3.18 page, confirm your reasoning on the auto-graded version of this exercise, and bring your best explanation to the class discussion.
partB.answer: (Hand-off to the auto-graded online exercise and the discussion — no written answer needed here.)

exercise2.title: Explain Why
exercise2.subtitle: 5 decision problems - classify and justify
exercise2.intro: Still wearing your Inspector's badge, you now write the REASONING behind each verdict. For each decision problem, decide whether it is decidable or undecidable and explain WHY in the CED's vocabulary. The judgments that trip people up are the ones that mix up impossibility with slowness, or that forget an undecidable problem can still have solvable instances. Vague answers like 'a computer can't do it' score zero — name the reason.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): confirm it is a decision problem (yes/no) + classify as decidable or undecidable + a correct one-sentence reason, for all 5 problems. Enrichment (optional): note a subtler point, such as a solvable instance or the contrast with unreasonable running time (EK AAP-4.B.3).
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every problem
method: Step 1 - Confirm it is a decision problem
Is the answer a plain yes or no? Decidable and undecidable are defined only for decision problems, so check this first.
Step 2 - Ask: could ONE algorithm be correct for ALL inputs?

If yes (you can name the algorithm), it is decidable. If it is an established result that no such algorithm can exist, it is undecidable. Do not try to PROVE undecidability yourself — rely on the definitions and well-known cases.
State the reason using 'algorithm,' 'all inputs,' and 'decidable/undecidable.' If the problem is undecidable, note whether particular instances can still be solved (EK AAP-4.B.3).
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (subtler judgment): The sharpest distinction to master is impossibility versus slowness. An undecidable problem has NO always-correct algorithm (EK AAP-4.B.2); a decidable-but-intractable problem has a correct algorithm that merely runs in an unreasonable amount of time (Topic 3.17). Also remember that undecidability is a property of the whole problem: some instances of an undecidable problem still have algorithmic solutions (EK AAP-4.B.3). Throughout, you apply established results — you are never asked to determine undecidability of a new problem yourself.
fields: Is this a decision problem (yes/no answer)?
Decidable or undecidable + a one-sentence reason:
Enrichment — a subtler point (solvable instance, or slow vs impossible):

scenarios.text: Problem: 'Is a given whole number positive?' You must answer yes or no for any integer someone provides.

key.signs: The answer is a plain yes or no, so it is a decision problem. One fixed rule settles it: check whether the number is greater than 0.
key.mechanism: This is DECIDABLE. A single algorithm — return whether the number > 0 — produces the correct output for all inputs (EK AAP-4.B.1). It works for 5 (yes), for -2 (no), for 0 (no), and for every integer, so the whole problem is decidable, exactly like 'Is the number even?'
key.subtype: Naming the algorithm is the proof: because one rule is correct for every input, no case can defeat it. This is the cleanest kind of decidable problem.

scenarios.text: Problem: 'Is a given word a palindrome (reads the same forward and backward)?' for any word provided.

key.signs: Yes/no answer, so it is a decision problem. A single algorithm handles any word: compare the first and last letters, then move inward, until they meet or a mismatch appears.
key.mechanism: This is DECIDABLE. The compare-from-both-ends algorithm gives the correct yes/no for all inputs (EK AAP-4.B.1) — 'racecar' returns yes, 'hello' returns no, and any word is handled by the same rule. Because one algorithm is correct for every input, the problem is decidable.
key.subtype: Even long words just mean more comparisons, not a different rule. 'More steps' is about time, not decidability — the algorithm still exists and is always correct.

scenarios.text: Problem: 'For ANY program and its input, will the program eventually stop, or will it run forever?' This is the Halting Problem, which computer scientists have PROVEN cannot be solved by any algorithm.

key.signs: It is a decision problem (halts: yes/no), but here you are TOLD the established result: no algorithm can decide it for all inputs. You apply that fact — you do not have to prove it (respecting the AAP-4.B.2 exclusion).
key.mechanism: This is UNDECIDABLE. By EK AAP-4.B.2, no algorithm can be constructed that always gives a correct yes-or-no answer for every program and input. It is an impossibility result, not a matter of the algorithm being slow or not yet discovered — no correct-for-all-inputs algorithm exists.
key.subtype: Note the exclusion in action: this is given to you as the canonical example of an undecidable problem. Your job is to EXPLAIN that it is undecidable and what that means, not to determine or prove the undecidability yourself.

scenarios.text: Problem instance: 'Does this exact program halt? — number ← 0; REPEAT 3 TIMES { number ← number + 1 }; DISPLAY(number).' Just this one specific program.

key.signs: Yes/no answer about a SINGLE, specific program — this is one instance of the Halting Problem, not the whole problem. Read it: the loop runs exactly 3 times and stops, so the answer is yes, it halts.
key.mechanism: This particular INSTANCE is solvable: you can trace the fixed loop and see it halts. That does not contradict the Halting Problem being undecidable. EK AAP-4.B.3 says an undecidable problem may have some instances with an algorithmic solution, but there is no algorithm that solves ALL instances.
key.subtype: Keep the level straight: instance versus whole problem. Solving this one program says nothing about being able to solve every program — which is the impossible part.

scenarios.text: Problem: 'Given a list of cities, is there a route visiting all of them whose total distance is below a given limit?' A correct algorithm exists (check every possible ordering), but for many cities it would take an astronomically long time.

key.signs: Yes/no answer, so it is a decision problem. Crucially, a correct algorithm DOES exist — try every possible ordering and compare — so this is not an impossibility case.
key.mechanism: This is DECIDABLE. Because an algorithm exists that is correct for all inputs (EK AAP-4.B.1), the problem is decidable even though that algorithm is impractically slow. Slowness is a Topic 3.17 concern (unreasonable running time), NOT undecidability.
key.subtype: This is the headline trap: 'takes forever' is not the same as 'undecidable.' Here a correct algorithm exists but runs in unreasonable time (so heuristics are used in practice); an undecidable problem has no correct-for-all-inputs algorithm at all.

discussion.title: Can Every Question Be Answered by an Algorithm?
discussion.minutes: ~20 min total - 3 parts - Opening + Mid-lesson + Closing (all within Day 1)
discussion.overview: A computer can settle 'Is this number even?' with a rule so simple a child could follow it — yet there are yes/no questions that NO algorithm can ever answer correctly for every case. Across three short windows today, your group will argue about where that line falls, why 'impossible' is different from 'slow,' and why an undecidable problem can still have answers for particular cases. This is the capstone conversation of Big Idea 3.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Your 'Yes/No Machine' could always answer 'Is the number even?' but not 'Will these instructions ever stop?' What, exactly, was different about the second question that made a foolproof rule impossible to write?
Question 2. Before today, would you have assumed that a powerful enough computer can answer any yes/no question if you just give it enough time? What would it mean for that assumption to be false?
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the undecidable-problems section
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - Impossible vs slow
items.text: In one sentence your whole group agrees on, state the difference between a problem that is UNDECIDABLE and one that just takes an UNREASONABLE amount of time. Then name which of the two still has a correct algorithm.

items.label: Task B - Whole problem vs one case
items.text: The Halting Problem is undecidable, yet you can easily tell that 'DISPLAY(1)' halts. Debate for about 3 minutes, then write the clearest explanation your group can give for how BOTH facts are true at once, using the words 'instance' and 'all inputs.'

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after Common AP Traps
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The two walls of computing
items.text: Big Idea 3 ends with two limits: some problems have no reasonable-time algorithm (Topic 3.17), and some have no algorithm at all (Topic 3.18). As a group, write a one-paragraph explanation a nervous classmate would actually understand of how these two limits differ — and why it is reassuring that most everyday problems hit NEITHER wall.
Our group's verdict (the single sentence we think best captures why 'undecidable' is not the same as 'too slow'):
